mcqs.question: Which memory management technique divides physical memory into fixed-size blocks called frames?
mcqs.explanation: Paging divides both physical memory (into frames) and logical memory (into pages) into fixed-size blocks. This eliminates external fragmentation.

mcqs.question: What type of fragmentation is caused by paging?
mcqs.explanation: Paging causes internal fragmentation because pages are fixed-size and may not fully utilize the last page allocated to a process.

mcqs.question: In the FIFO page replacement algorithm, which page is replaced when a page fault occurs?
mcqs.explanation: FIFO (First In First Out) evicts the page that has been in memory the longest, treating memory like a queue.

mcqs.question: What happens during thrashing?

options.text: CPU is idle due to lack of processes
Excessive page faults cause high paging I/O and low CPU utilization
Processes are being swapped out completely

mcqs.explanation: Thrashing occurs when the system spends more time page faulting and swapping pages than executing processes, causing severe performance degradation.

mcqs.question: Which component translates virtual addresses to physical addresses?
mcqs.explanation: The MMU is hardware that performs address translation using page tables, converting virtual addresses used by programs to physical addresses in RAM.

mcqs.question: What is the purpose of a Translation Lookaside Buffer (TLB)?

options.text: To cache recent virtual-to-physical address translations

mcqs.explanation: TLB is a hardware cache that stores recent page table entries, speeding up virtual-to-physical address translation.

mcqs.question: Which page replacement algorithm provides the optimal (lowest) page fault rate but is impractical to implement?
mcqs.explanation: The Optimal algorithm replaces the page that will not be used for the longest time in the future, giving the lowest faults but requiring future knowledge.

mcqs.question: In demand paging, when are pages loaded into memory?
mcqs.explanation: Demand paging loads pages only when they are referenced, deferring page loading until actually needed by the executing process.

mcqs.question: What is the working set of a process?

options.text: The set of pages the process is currently using
The total memory allocated to the process

mcqs.explanation: The working set is the set of pages a process has referenced in the recent past. Keeping the working set in memory minimizes page faults.

mcqs.question: Which technique allows execution of programs larger than physical memory?
mcqs.explanation: Virtual memory extends physical memory using disk space, allowing programs larger than RAM to execute by swapping pages to/from disk.

mcqs.question: What causes external fragmentation?

options.text: Scattered free memory blocks after allocation/deallocation

mcqs.explanation: External fragmentation occurs when free memory becomes divided into small non-contiguous blocks, making it impossible to allocate a large block despite sufficient total free memory.

mcqs.question: What is the main advantage of segmentation over paging?

options.text: Provides logical view of memory with variable-sized segments

mcqs.explanation: Segmentation provides a logical view of memory by dividing programs into meaningful segments (code, data, stack) of variable sizes.

mcqs.question: In a two-level page table system, what does each entry of the first-level table contain?

options.text: Pointer to a second-level page table

mcqs.explanation: Each first-level page table entry contains a pointer (or reference) to a second-level page table, creating a hierarchical structure.

mcqs.question: What is the page fault rate when a process starts execution?

options.text: Very low - only some pages needed
Very high - demand paging loads pages as referenced

mcqs.explanation: Initially, no pages are in memory, so every memory reference causes a page fault until the working set is loaded, resulting in high initial fault rate.

mcqs.question: Which memory allocation scheme uses variable-sized partitions?
mcqs.explanation: Dynamic or variable partitioning allocates memory based on exact process size, creating partitions that match the exact memory requirement.

mcqs.question: What is memory compaction used for?

options.text: To eliminate external fragmentation by moving allocated blocks

mcqs.explanation: Compaction moves allocated memory blocks to gather all free memory into one contiguous block, eliminating external fragmentation.

mcqs.question: What is the key difference between LRU and FIFO page replacement?

options.text: LRU uses reference bits, FIFO does not
LRU tracks page usage history, FIFO treats pages as queue
LRU is hardware-implemented, FIFO is software

mcqs.explanation: LRU (Least Recently Used) tracks when pages were last accessed and evicts the least recently used page. FIFO simply tracks arrival order in memory.

mcqs.question: What happens when all frames in memory are occupied and a new page must be loaded?

options.text: A page replacement algorithm selects a victim page to evict

mcqs.explanation: When all frames are full, a page replacement algorithm must select a page to evict (victim) to make room for the newly referenced page.

mcqs.question: Which of the following is NOT a valid page replacement algorithm?
mcqs.explanation: SCAN is a disk scheduling algorithm, not a page replacement algorithm. FIFO, LRU, and Priority (though less common) are valid page replacement strategies.

mcqs.question: What is theBelady's Anomaly related to?

options.text: FIFO page replacement showing more faults with more frames

mcqs.explanation: Belady's Anomaly is the counterintuitive phenomenon where increasing the number of page frames can actually increase page faults in FIFO algorithm.
